title: Italian Bishop Mirror
setup: White has played the Italian opening, placing a bishop on **c4** aimed at f7. Black mirrors the threat.
hint: When your opponent develops a bishop attacking your weak square, develop *your* bishop attacking theirs.
explanation:
## The Idea

This is a **classical development** decision in the Italian Game. White's bishop on c4 stares down Black's f7-square. Black should respond by mirroring: develop the bishop to **c5**, eyeing White's symmetrical weak square — **f2**.

Black plays {{Bc5}}.

## Why c5?

- The bishop is **active**: it attacks the long a7-g1 diagonal, including the f2 square (defended only by White's king).
- The bishop is **flexible**: it doesn't block any other piece's development.
- The move is **symmetric**: matching White's threat with an equal threat is a mature opening idea.

## What This Sets Up

This is the entry into the **Giuoco Piano** ("Quiet Game") or the more aggressive **Giuoco Pianissimo** lines. Both sides have:

- An active bishop on the long diagonal.
- Both knights developed.
- Quick castling on the way.

## Pattern to Memorize

> Develop your bishops to **active squares that attack key enemy weaknesses** — typically f2/f7 in the opening.

Don't waste opening tempo on cosmetic moves. Every developing move should improve your position **and** put pressure on the opponent. Bc5 does both in one stroke.
